sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 18, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves active green frame right by 3 cols VERIFIED', 'LEFT': 'Moves active green frame left by 3 cols VERIFIED', 'UP': 'Moves active green frame up by 3 rows VERIFIED', 'DOWN': 'Moves active green frame down by 3 rows VERIFIED', 'MOUSE': 'Clicking on green frame selects it (gives white center), clicking on center pixel changes its color VERIFIED'}
- Object taxonomy: {'g': 'gray background', 'w': 'white panels/squares', 'p': 'purple vertical bar (barrier between panels)', 'N': 'light green 3x3 frames (movable tokens)', 'c': 'charcoal 5x5 frames (targets)', 'W': 'white single pixel (selected token center)', 'B': 'black single pixel (unselected token center)'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'row 63, charcoal strip'}
- Win pattern: {'rule': 'Move each green frame into a charcoal target frame. Select frame with MOUSE, then use arrow keys to move it.'}
Current level:
- World model: Right frame active (white center). Currently at rows 30-32, cols 27-29. Just executed UP+6xRIGHT. Need to check if frame reached right target at rows 26-30, cols 44-48. Left frame at rows 30-32, cols 18-20 with charcoal center (not active). Left target at rows 32-36, cols 11-15.
- Goal model: Move green frames into charcoal target frames. Right frame needs to go to right target (rows 26-30, cols 44-48). Left frame needs to go to left target (rows 32-36, cols 11-15).
- Recent findings: ['MOUSE selects active frame (gives white center)', 'Arrow keys move active frame by 3 pixels', 'RIGHT frame moved right with RIGHT key', 'UP moves frame up by 3 rows', 'Batch of UP+6xRIGHT just executed - need to verify result']
- Open questions: ['Can frames pass through purple barrier?', 'Does the frame need to be centered in the target?', 'What happens when frame reaches target?']
- Plan: Check if right frame reached target. If yes, select left frame and move it to left target. If not, adjust movement.
- Strategy log: ['Initial exploration: tested RIGHT/LEFT/UP/DOWN on left frame (steps 1-6). Discovered MOUSE selection mechanic (step 7). Moved right frame toward target (steps 8-10).']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 17 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The UP action didn't move the frame - it's still at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. Looking at the ascii:
- Row 21-25: the purple barrier (pppppp) is at cols 34-39
- Row 30: the purple barrier is at cols 33-38
- The barrier extends from row 21 to at least row 39
The frame is stuck because:
1. UP doesn't work - probably blocked by the white panel boundary or the barrier
2. RIGHT doesn't work - blocked by the purple barrier
Wait, but the frame WAS able to move RIGHT once (from cols 27-29 to 30-32). So it can move within the left panel but can't cross the barrier.
Looking more carefully at the layout:
- The left white panel is at cols ~10-29 (approximately)
- The purple barrier is at cols 33-38
- The right white panel is at cols 39+
The right green frame started at cols 27-29 (in the left panel), moved RIGHT once to cols 30-32, but then got blocked by the barrier at cols 33-38.
The key question: can the frame ever cross to the right panel? Looking at the ascii, the barrier seems continuous from row 21 to row 39+. There's no obvious gap.
Wait - maybe the right green frame is NOT supposed to go to the right target. Maybe the left frame goes to the left target and the right frame stays in the left panel? Or maybe there's a different mechanic I'm missing.
Let me reconsider. Looking at the initial setup:
- Left panel: left target (charcoal frame) + left green frame + right green frame
- Right panel: right target (charcoal frame)
Maybe the right green frame needs to go to the LEFT target, not the right target? That would make more sense since it's already in the left panel.
Or maybe both frames need to go to the left target? That doesn't make sense either.
Let me re-examine. The left target is at rows 32-36, cols 11-15. The left green frame is at rows 30-32, cols 18-20. To get the left frame to the left target:
- Need to move LEFT (from cols 18-20 to cols 12-14 to center in 11-15)
- Need to move DOWN (from rows 30-32 to rows 33-35 to center in 32-36)
For the right green frame, if it's supposed to go to the left target too, that doesn't make sense. But if it's supposed to go somewhere else...
Wait, maybe I should reconsider the goal. Maybe the green frames need to overlap the charcoal targets, and the right frame is supposed to go to the left target while the left frame... hmm.
Actually, let me look at this differently. Maybe the right green frame should go to the left target (since it can't cross the barrier), and the left green frame should... also go to the left target? That doesn't work.
Or maybe the goal is different - maybe the green frames need to be moved so that their centers align with something, or maybe they need to be moved to specific positions within their panels.
Let me try a different approach: move the left frame to the left target and see what happens. If the level completes, then the right frame might not need to move at all, or maybe it needs to go somewhere else.
Actually wait - let me re-read the ascii more carefully. The right green frame is at cols 30-32 in the current frame. The left panel's right edge seems to be around col 29 (based on row 30: `wwwwwNNNpppppp...` where the white panel ends before the purple barrier). So the frame is actually partially overlapping with the barrier area.
Hmm, but the frame moved there via RIGHT action. So maybe the barrier doesn't block the frame - maybe the frame can overlap with it?
Let me try RIGHT again to see if the frame can move further right, past the barrier.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The frame is stuck at cols 30-32. Let me try RIGHT again to see if it can pass the barrier
# Also check if maybe the frame can go through the barrier
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
n_frames = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'N']
for n in n_frames:
    print(f"N frame: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try RIGHT to see if frame can pass barrier
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>

UP didn't work. Need to figure out how to cross barrier or reconsider goal.", "goal_model": "Move green frames into charcoal target frames - but right frame can't reach right target due to barrier", "recent_findings": ["Purple barrier blocks horizontal movement", "UP also blocked"], "open_questions": ["Can frame pass through barrier?", "Is the goal different than I thought?"], "current_plan": "Try RIGHT again to test barrier permeability", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["UP on right frame (blocked)"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
